title: Shavuot: The Night We Stayed Up to Receive the Torah

sources.title: What Is Tikkun Leil Shavuot? - All-night learning
sources.description: Comprehensive explanation of the custom of staying up all night on Shavuot, including its origins, meanings, and practical halachic details
key_quotes: On the first night of Shavuot, we stay up all night learning Torah. Known as 'tikkun,' this Kabbalah-inspired custom is a preparation to receive the Torah the following morning when the 10 Commandments are read.
The Jewish people were so excited about receiving the Torah that they went to sleep the night before and slept perfectly, without a sound. And slept in. Moses had to wake them up.

Their mistake was that, by going to sleep, the Jews demonstrated that they had completely misunderstood the point of the Torah. The Torah wasn't given to us so that we can become spiritual beings, devoid of all vestiges of physicality.

We stay up to show God that we haven't missed the point. We could opt to go to sleep, to cleave to the spiritual and ignore our physical body. But instead, we spend the night learning, working with our body, inspiring it, purifying it.

sources.title: Origins of Tikkun Leil Shavuot
sources.description: Source sheet with original Jewish texts about the custom of staying awake on Shavuot night
key_quotes: Anyone who doesn't sleep at all on this night, and engages in Torah - it's guaranteed that he will live out the year and no harm will befall him, and the decree of a person's life is on this night.

sources.title: Up All Night
sources.description: Personal essay about studying Torah all night on Shavuot and the spiritual significance of nighttime learning
key_quotes: On Shavuot, which commemorates the giving of the Torah on Mount Sinai, we stay up all night to study the Torah to celebrate. We express our enthusiasm for the Torah by learning it from dusk until dawn.
The Talmud teaches, 'One who studies Torah at night, a strand of loving kindness is drawn over him during the day' (Avodah Zara 3b).
During the daytime a person may be studying so that others see him/her, but nighttime learning takes place in modesty and seclusion. It's an expression of Torah learning for its own sake.

sources.title: Ruth 1:16-17
sources.description: Ruth's famous declaration of commitment to Judaism, read on Shavuot
key_quotes: But Ruth replied, 'Do not urge me to leave you, to turn back and not follow you. For wherever you go, I will go; wherever you lodge, I will lodge; your people shall be my people, and your God my God. Where you die, I will die, and there I will be buried.'
